result.markdown: --- title: 'I am become Gerund, Destroyer of Verbs' date: '2023-08-09' categories: ['oppenheimer', 'llm', 'ai', 'Sanskrit', 'bhagavad-gita', 'anthropic', 'linguistics'] description: 'In which we explore Sanskrit with AI, using Oppenheimer as an excuse.' execute: message: false warning: false echo: true editor_options: chunk_output_type: console image: "angkor-wat.jpg" bibliography: citations.bib --- ![Radiance of a thousand suns: Sun rise at Angkor Wat. Photo by [Sebastian Latorre](https://unsplash.com/@sebalatorre?utm_source=unsplash&utm_medium=referral&utm_content=creditCopyText) on [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com/photos/lrG4nki2kYM?utm_source=unsplash&utm_medium=referral&utm_content=creditCopyText) ](./angkor-wat.jpg){#fig-hero fig-align="center"} ## 0. Executive Summary You may have watched in Christopher Nolan's new movie. Or you may have read it in the [2005 biography](https://archive.org/details/americanpromethe00bird/mode/1up) [@bird2005american] on which the movie is based. Or may have known it all along. But [Julius Robert Oppenheimer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J._Robert_Oppenheimer) is famously said to have ~~quoted~~ thought of a quote from the _Bhagavad Gitā_ upon witnessing the first nuclear explosion: > I am become Death, the destroyer of worlds. Can we translate the original verse differently? Let's use AI to find out. ### The Quote Per his brother Frank's recollection, Oppenheimer's actual words at seeing the first nuclear explosion on 16 Jul 1945 at Alamogordo, New Mexico were: _I guess it worked_ . It was only later in 1949 [@barnett1949j] when he was interviewed for Life magazine that he said he thought of the following verse from the _Gitā_ : > If the radiance of a thousand suns were to burst at once into the sky, that would be like the splendor of the mighty one ... Now I am become Death, the shatterer of worlds. Oppenheimer was always interested in "the mystical and cryptic" because he liked to learn things that were "difficult", with science being reasonably easy. [@bird2005american, pp. 99, 102] Which is why he learnt Sanskrit, and apparently read not just the _Bhagavad Gitā_, but also Kālidāsa's 4th century lyrical poem _Méghadūta_, in the original Sanskrit. Which is why he seemingly translated the verse(s) himself. Can we do so as well? We'll need a crash course in Sanskrit to get started. ## Sanskrit: The Language I haven't had a formal education in Sanskrit. But it is a _liturgical_ language that I chant in every day, not just for Hindu slokas from the _Gitā_ and the Vedas, but also for Buddhist sutras such as the _Prajñaparamita Hrdaya Sūtra_. A lot of Sanskrit words have made their way into contemporary languages in both South and South East Asia, so I do understand many words. ### Same-same but different But those familiar waters can sometimes be treacherous. The same word could have different meanings in Sanskrit and other languages. [False Friends](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/False_friend) is a Thing, more so in Indic languages than in other language families. I often need to rely on guard-rails such as [Sanskrit Dictionary Collections](https://andhrabharati.com/dictionary/sanskrit/index.php) to keep me safe. Then there are [sandhi's](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sandhi), liasons between words to form compound words. Whilst the individual word may be understandable, the compound word could feel and sound new; you will need to know how the sandhi has been formed in order to untangle the compound word into its constituents. ### Same-same not same as sama-sama Sanskrit has been historically written in range of scripts, down from the earliest [Brahmi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brahmi_script) and [Kharosthi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kharosthi) scripts, to more contemporary scripts across Asia. Here's a good example of how it all intersects. Consider this quote from Kālidāsa mentioned in Wikipedia, written in various Asian scripts: ![_'May Śiva bless those who take delight in the language of gods'_ - Kālidāsa. (From[Wikipedia](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Phrase_sanskrit.svg))](Phrase_sanskrit.svg){#fig-sanskrit fig-align="center"} Sanskrit is written in each of these scripts for liturgical needs in South and South East Asia. The first word is easily understood: it clearly refers to Siva, one of the gods in the Hindu pantheon. The second word is also easily understood: it's clearly a conjugation of the word _raksha_ or _to protect_. But wait: how did _to protect_ become _bless_? It gets more difficult with the next word, a multi-letter monster in every script mentioned in the graphic. It is a _compound_ word: among others, words meaning 'language', 'god' and 'delight' have been somehow strung together to form a single word. This is where it starts to become complex. ### Haksara Wrehastra: Conjunct Letters For, untangling conjunct words is not just a matter of giving the right amount of spaces. Most Brahmi-originated scripts have [conjunct letters](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abugida#General_description), complex rules where letters may be combined in certain combinations to form new letters. 36 consonants can be combined to form [1296 two-letter combinations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Devanagari_conjuncts) in Sanskrit, but that is not all. Each of these combinations can be combined with 16 vowels to form even more complex conjuncts. There is no grammatical limit to the number of letters you can combine. At the most extreme, the word कार्त्स्न्य ("entirety") consists of a five-letter conjunct, र्त्स्न्य [@stiehl2017devanagari]. Whilst I can read many of the scripts listed in [@fig-sanskrit], I find it easier break conjunct vowels and therefore words into their constituent parts in Telugu script. But that may not be your experience; perhaps you're used to reading about Ghatōtgacha's exploits in Balinese script. Which brings us to our first computational challenge: can we transliterate a Sanskrit string into a script of our choice? ## 1. Writing Sanskrit ### 1.1 Methods for Transliteration ::: {.callout-note} Transliteration is to convert a string from one script to another without changing the word. This is different from translation, which is to convert a string from one language to another. ::: A naive suggestion could be that an LLM could draft us a quick method in transliterate Telugu strings to Sanskrit. We tried to get Anthropic Claude 2 to do just that: ```javascript import { Observable } from 'observable-fns' const teluguToDevanagari = Observable.create((teluguText) => { const mappings = { 'అ':'अ', 'ఆ':'आ', 'ఇ':'इ', 'ఈ':'ई', 'ఉ':'उ', 'ఊ':'ऊ', 'ఋ':'ऋ', 'ఌ':'ऌ', 'ఎ':'ए', 'ఏ':'ऐ', 'ఐ':'ऑ', 'ఒ':'ओ', 'ఓ':'औ', 'ఔ':'अउ', // ...add remaining mappings } let devanagariText = '' for(let i=0; i{"contents": [{"name": "scriptRanges", "value": {"assamese": [[2432, 2559]], "bengali": [[2432, 2559]], "devanagari": [[2304, 2431], [43232, 43263]], "gujarati": [[2688, 2815]], "gurmukhi": [[2560, 2687]], "oriya": [[2816, 2943]], "tibetan": [[3840, 4095]], "tamil": [[2944, 3071]], "telugu": [[3072, 3199], [3200, 3327]], "kannada": [[3200, 3327]], "malayalam": [[3328, 3455]], "sinhala": [[3456, 3583]], "thai": [[3584, 3711]], "lao": [[3712, 3839]], "myanmar": [[4096, 4255]], "khmer": [[6016, 6143], [6624, 6655]], "balinese": [[6912, 7039]], "javanese": [[43392, 43487]], "sundanese": [[7040, 7103]]}}]} ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html} ``` ::: ::: ```{ojs} //| echo: false //print script_dict viewof txtInput = Inputs.text({label: html`Input Text`, placeholder: "Enter text here (e.g. సంస్కృతం)"}); viewof scripts = Inputs.select(clean_scripts, {label: html`Target Script`, placeholder: "Select target script"}); ```
